Assistant at Hartford High School (successor to Hopkins Grammar School). Met William Capron there.
In charge of Madura Girls' Boarding School on arrival 1857. Continued mission work alone 1876-1886 after husband's death. Did evangelical, medical, and educational work.

career.notes: Superintendent of Women's Department. The institute later became the Moody Bible Institute.

education.notes: Attended Wheaton Female Seminary per family history.

education.notes: Attended State Normal School at West Newton MA.

institutions.institution_blurb: Teacher at Hartford High School (successor to Hopkins Grammar School).
Served under ABCFM at Madura mission 1857-1886.
Superintendent of Women's Department of the Bible Institute for Home and Foreign Missions (later Moody Bible Institute) 1889-1894.

cross_connections.display_label: Her uncle John moved to Chicago in 1836; she went to India in 1856
Husband Capron served the mission Thompson's deputation inspected

narrative_blocks.header: Ran the Madura Girls' Boarding School
narrative_blocks.body: On arriving at Madurai in 1857, Sarah Brown Hooker was immediately put in charge of the Madura Girls' Boarding School while her husband William performed evangelical work and supervised construction at Manamadurai. She had met William while both were teaching at the Hartford High School in Connecticut — she was appointed to the mission just weeks after their October 1856 marriage.

narrative_blocks.header: Her Yale missionary husband died in India, 1876
narrative_blocks.body: Rev. William Banfield Capron died of heart disease at Madura on October 6, 1876, leaving Sarah a widow with two surviving daughters far from Massachusetts — Annie and Laura remained in the United States under the care of Sarah's sister Anne. He was fifty-two; they had been married twenty years.

narrative_blocks.header: Remained at the mission alone for ten years
narrative_blocks.body: After William's death in 1876, Sarah Brown Hooker chose to remain at the Madurai mission alone — doing evangelical, medical, and educational work in Manamadurai and Madurai until 1886, a decade of solitary service in South India. She finally returned to the United States after thirty years abroad.

narrative_blocks.header: Superintendent at the Bible Institute that became Moody's
narrative_blocks.body: In 1889, Dwight L. Moody personally asked Sarah to become superintendent of the Women's Department at his new Bible Institute in Chicago; when she questioned her fitness, he replied, 'It is the experience of life that I want.' She served until 1894, then retired to Boston, conducted a Bible class at Old South Church, and eventually died in Poughkeepsie on December 15, 1918 at ninety.

She had met William while both were teaching at the Hartford High School in Connecticut — she was appointed to the mission just weeks after their October 1856 marriage.

William Banfield Capron died of heart disease at Madura on October 6, 1876, leaving Sarah a widow with two surviving daughters far from Massachusetts — Annie and Laura remained in the United States under the care of Sarah's sister Anne. He was fifty-two; they had been married twenty years.

career.notes: One year as private tutor in Baltimore after Yale graduation.
Six years as principal. School later merged into Hartford High School. Where he met Sarah Brown Hooker.
Sailed November 21, 1856; arrived Madras March 6, 1857. Sixteen years in vicinity of Madura. Visited U.S. 1872; returned to India 1874. Died of heart disease Madura October 6, 1876.

education.institution_blurb: Preliminary course at Phillips Academy, Andover MA.
Graduated Yale College 1846. Private tutor in Baltimore one year after graduation.
Graduated Andover Theological Seminary 1856; ordained September 3, 1856 at Uxbridge MA.

institutions.institution_blurb: Andover Theological Seminary, graduated and ordained 1856.
Principal, Hopkins Grammar School Hartford CT, six years.
ABCFM missionary at Madura South India 1857-1876.

narrative_blocks.header: Yale and Andover
narrative_blocks.body: William Banfield Capron graduated Yale College in 1846 and spent six years as principal of Hopkins Grammar School in Hartford, Connecticut — where he met Sarah Brown Hooker, who was teaching there. After graduating from Andover Theological Seminary in 1856, he was ordained September 3 and sailed for India that November.

narrative_blocks.header: Sailed for India 1856
narrative_blocks.body: Under the American Board of Commissioners for Foreign Missions, Capron sailed November 21, 1856, arrived at Madras March 6, 1857, and labored for sixteen years in the vicinity of Madura in South India. He visited America in 1872 and returned to the mission in 1874; he died of heart disease at Madura on October 6, 1876, at fifty-two.

narrative_blocks.header: Three Capron children all born in India
narrative_blocks.body: Three children were born to William and Sarah at the mission in India — Annie Hooker in 1860, Laura Elizabeth in 1862, and Henry, who was born in 1864 and died in 1865 as an infant. His brother Samuel Mills Capron had succeeded him as principal of Hopkins Grammar School, and died the same year William returned to India.

narrative_blocks.header: Born in South India
narrative_blocks.body: Annie Hooker Capron was born September 17, 1860 at Madura in South India — the daughter of Rev. William Capron and Sarah Brown Hooker, who had followed the ABCFM mission to the subcontinent. She married Lewis Kennedy Morse on April 21, 1897 and died October 31, 1909 at forty-nine — leaving two children, Anna Hooker and Arthur Webster Morse.

naming_inspiration.description: Named Annie Hooker — born in India, honoring the Hooker lineage through her mother Sarah Brown Hooker.

career.notes: Boston attorney.
Treasurer of Wellesley College.

cross_connections.display_label: Grandson Lewis born in France; died at twenty, 1946

narrative_blocks.header: Boston attorney and treasurer of Wellesley College
narrative_blocks.body: Lewis Kennedy Morse practiced law in Boston and served as treasurer of Wellesley College — combining legal practice with educational stewardship in the Boston Brahmin tradition. He spent summers at the Pearson-Morse-Haynes house in Boxford, Massachusetts from 1904 until his death in 1930.

narrative_blocks.header: Married a woman born in India to missionaries
narrative_blocks.body: Annie Hooker Capron, whom Lewis married on April 21, 1897 in Boston, had been born in Madura, South India, where her parents Rev. William Capron and Sarah Brown Hooker were serving as ABCFM missionaries. When Annie died in October 1909, Lewis later married Ednah Anne Rich in San Francisco in July 1916.

narrative_blocks.header: Playground in Boxford was dedicated on Halloween, 1930
narrative_blocks.body: After Lewis's death on March 8, 1930, his widow Ednah Rich Morse donated his Boxford land adjacent to the new Aaron Wood School as a playground — dedicated October 31, 1930. A stone marker placed by local volunteers named it the Lewis Kennedy Morse Playground; the land is now part of Cole School grounds.

narrative_blocks.header: Mother died when she was ten
narrative_blocks.body: Anna Hooker Morse's mother Annie Hooker Capron died in October 1909 when Anna was just ten years old — born in India to missionaries, her mother died in her prime at forty-nine. Anna herself lived to ninety-three, dying May 10, 1992 in Boxford, Massachusetts.

narrative_blocks.header: Both sons born in France
narrative_blocks.body: Anna married Winthrop Perrin Haynes — who had served as a 2nd Lieutenant in World War I — in Boxford on August 10, 1925. Both their sons, Lewis Rogerson (1926) and Edward Hamilton (1928), were born in Neuilly-sur-Seine, France, where the family was then living.

narrative_blocks.header: Her father's playground is still a Boxford landmark
narrative_blocks.body: The Lewis Kennedy Morse Playground, donated posthumously by her stepmother Ednah Rich Morse and dedicated October 31, 1930, commemorated Anna's father on the grounds of what is now Cole School in Boxford. The Haynes family estate on Main Street — the Pearson-Morse-Haynes house — was named Elmlea and became the Elmlea Tree Farm.

naming_inspiration.description: Named Anna Hooker — honoring the Hooker lineage through her mother Annie Hooker Capron.

narrative_blocks.header: Died two days before his seventy-fifth birthday, 1975
narrative_blocks.body: Arthur Webster Morse was born March 9, 1900 and died March 7, 1975 in Stamford, Connecticut — two days before what would have been his seventy-fifth birthday. He was buried with his sister Anna at Village Cemetery in Boxford, Massachusetts.

marriages.notes: James Dyer Keith (1864-1943). Children: James Monroe Keith (1893-1982) and Annie Hooker Hart née Keith (1895-1969).

narrative_blocks.header: Died three days before her eighty-fourth birthday
narrative_blocks.body: Laura Elizabeth Capron Keith was born June 17, 1862 in India and died June 14, 1946 in Poughkeepsie — three days before what would have been her eighty-fourth birthday. Her mother Sarah Brown Hooker had lived with her in Poughkeepsie in her final years and died there in 1918.

narrative_blocks.header: Born in India to missionaries
narrative_blocks.body: Born at the ABCFM mission station in Madura, South India, Laura married James Dyer Keith in 1892 and settled in Poughkeepsie, where she and her husband were well known until their deaths. She outlived her mother by twenty-eight years and her sister Annie by thirty-seven.

narrative_blocks.header: Named Annie Hooker
narrative_blocks.body: Annie Hooker Keith carried the Hooker family name as her middle name — born June 29, 1895 at Hingham, Massachusetts, to James Dyer Keith and Laura Elizabeth Capron, who had been born in India to missionary parents. She married and became Annie Hooker Hart, living until 1969 at seventy-three.

naming_inspiration.description: Named Annie Hooker in honor of the Hooker lineage through her mother Laura Elizabeth Capron née Hooker.

narrative_blocks.header: Died in India at one year old, 1865
narrative_blocks.body: Henry Hooker Capron was born in 1864 at the Madurai mission in India and died in 1865 — living less than a year. Named to honor both his grandfather Rev. Henry Brown Hooker and the wider Hooker family lineage, he was the only one of the Capron children born in India who did not survive childhood.

naming_inspiration.description: Named Henry Hooker — honoring grandfather Rev. Henry Brown Hooker (H01418).

career.notes: One year as home missionary in South Carolina after ordination October 10, 1825.
May 2, 1827 to May 17, 1836 — about 10 years.
February 1837 to June 1858 — about 21 years. Obituary says 'about 10 years' — likely referring to active full-time pastorate.
Secretary 1858-1873; succeeded Rev. Dr. Clark. Continued assisting the society until death July 4, 1881.
Member Massachusetts Board of Education 1844-1851.

education.notes: Graduated Middlebury College 1821. Obituary source: Middlebury Register July 8, 1881.

education.institution_blurb: Graduated Andover Theological Seminary 1825; ordained October 10, 1825.

institutions.institution_blurb: Corporate member from 1845; daughter Sarah was ABCFM missionary in India.
American Tract Society published eight tracts from Rev. Hooker's pen.
Secretary of Massachusetts Home Missionary Society 1858-1873.

narrative_blocks.header: Secretary of Massachusetts Home Missionary Society, 1858-1873
narrative_blocks.body: Rev. Henry Brown Hooker served as secretary of the Massachusetts Home Missionary Society from 1858 to 1873, succeeding Rev. Dr. Clark — capping a ministry that had included pastorates in South Carolina, Lanesboro, and Falmouth, Massachusetts. He continued assisting the society until his death on July 4, 1881, Independence Day, in Boston at seventy-eight.

narrative_blocks.header: American Tract Society published eight of his tracts
narrative_blocks.body: The American Tract Society published eight tracts from Rev. Hooker's pen, and he wrote three more for the Tract Society of Boston, as well as two Sunday-school books — 'Plea for the Heathen' and 'Put Off and Put On' — that had large sales. His obituary noted that his contributions to the religious press were 'frequent and popular.'

narrative_blocks.header: Twin who chose ministry while his brother John
narrative_blocks.body: Born the same day as his twin John Worthington Hooker on August 31, 1802, Henry took the path of Congregational ministry while John moved to Chicago's frontier in 1836. Henry's daughter Sarah Brown Hooker would carry the evangelical tradition a generation further — thirty years as a missionary to South India.

narrative_blocks.header: ABCFM corporate member from 1845
narrative_blocks.body: From 1845, Rev. Hooker served as a corporate member of the American Board of Commissioners for Foreign Missions — the same organization his son-in-law Rev. William Banfield Capron would serve as a missionary in South India from 1857. His daughter Sarah spent thirty years at the ABCFM mission in Madura, a direct extension of her father's decades of institutional commitment.

narrative_blocks.header: Outlived her husband by twelve years
narrative_blocks.body: Martha Vinal Chickering married Henry Brown Hooker on May 28, 1827 and outlived him by twelve years, dying at Roxbury, Massachusetts, November 16, 1893. She was the daughter of Asa W. and Anna Hart (Titcomb) Chickering.

career.notes: Per EH 1909 #530: 'an apothecary and grocer at Rutland, Vt.'

narrative_blocks.header: Apothecary and grocer at Rutland; wife predeceased him
narrative_blocks.body: Thomas Hooker was an apothecary and grocer at Rutland, Vermont, where he spent his adult life and died in 1836 at sixty-five. His wife Sarah Brown died four years before him in 1832, predeceasing him despite being nine years younger.

narrative_blocks.header: Married daughter of Col. John Brown of Pittsfield
narrative_blocks.body: Sarah Brown's father, Colonel John Brown of Pittsfield, Massachusetts, is almost certainly the Revolutionary War officer killed at the Battle of Stone Arabia, New York, on October 19, 1780 — when Sarah was barely a year old. If confirmed, Thomas Hooker married the daughter of a Revolutionary War martyr.

narrative_blocks.header: Twin sons: one to Boston missions
narrative_blocks.body: John Worthington and Henry Brown Hooker were born the same day — August 31, 1802 — and their lives diverged completely: John moved to Chicago in 1836 among the city's earliest New England settlers, while Henry graduated Middlebury College, entered the ministry, and became Secretary of Missions at Boston.

narrative_blocks.header: Two of five children died in Chicago
narrative_blocks.body: John Worthington Hooker moved to Chicago in 1836 and died there in 1866; his sister Julia Ann married a Rutland lawyer and also died in Chicago in 1842. Two of Thomas Hooker's five children ended their days in the city that barely existed when they were born.

narrative_blocks.header: Probably daughter of a Revolutionary War martyr
narrative_blocks.body: Sarah Brown's father, Colonel John Brown of Pittsfield Massachusetts named in the Hooker genealogy, is almost certainly the Revolutionary War officer killed at the Battle of Stone Arabia, New York, on October 19, 1780 — when Sarah was barely a year old. She married Thomas Hooker in 1796 and died at Rutland, Vermont, in 1832, four years before her husband.

INST001.name: Hopkins Grammar School
INST001.notes:
Founded by bequest of Edward Hopkins, Governor of Connecticut and overseer of Thomas Hooker's will. Hopkins also named Mary Newton (H00003) and Sarah Wilson (H00006) in his own will with direct bequests.
[founder] Edward Hopkins

INST041.primary_name: American Board of Commissioners for Foreign Missions (ABCFM)
INST041.name: American Board of Commissioners for Foreign Missions (ABCFM)
INST041.notes: Founded 1810 at Andover Seminary, Massachusetts by prominent Congregationalists. First American foreign missionary society; first missions to China and Ceylon. Yale President Rev. Timothy Dwight IV (H00914) was among the founding figures; Dwight Mission (established 1820 in present-day Arkansas/Oklahoma for the Cherokee) was named in his honor. The board operated Brainerd Mission (Chattanooga TN, est.1817) where Harriet Ellsworth Blunt (X02121) and Ainsworth Blunt (X02122) served the Cherokee. HOOKER TREE CONNECTIONS: 1. Rev. Timothy Dwight IV (H00914) — Yale President 1795-1817; founding ABCFM figure; Dwight Mission named for him. 5th-generation Hooker descendant. 2. Elias Cornelius (X00724) — ABCFM Secretary of Domestic Correspondence 1819-1832; conducted 1817 missionary tour to Creek and Cherokee; m. Mary Ann Hooker (H01355). 3. Rev. Edward William Hooker (H01354) — corporate ABCFM member from 1840. 4. Harriet Ellsworth Blunt (X02121) — served at Brainerd Mission 1821-1837; granddaughter of Rev. Cyprian Strong (H00299). 5. Ainsworth Blunt (X02122) — Brainerd missionary 1822-1837; accompanied Cherokee on Trail of Tears 1838; first mayor of Dalton GA. 6. Dr. Mary Floyd Cushman (HD2818, gen 8) — ABCFM medical missionary in Angola 1922-1953. 7. Samuel Gelston Dwight (HD2376) — ABCFM missionary to Hawaii; children named for ABCFM colleagues Juliette Cooke and Samuel Castle.

INST151.primary_name: Bible Institute for Home and Foreign Missions (later Moody Bible Institute)
INST151.name: Bible Institute for Home and Foreign Missions (later Moody Bible Institute)
INST151.notes: H02770 Sarah Brown Hooker Capron served as superintendent of the Women's Department 1889-1894. The institution later became the Moody Bible Institute, named for Dwight L. Moody.
